data.title: Duolingo: AI Advantage, Turning A Headwind Into A Tailwind
data.description:
This is my first cover of Duolingo (NASDAQ:DUOL). In the article, I will describe its business and how I believe AI will influence its future. Based on that, I will try to provide a reasonable valuation and try to justify why I believe it is currently a strong buy.

Introduction to Duolingo

As most of you already know, Duolingo is a language learning app that is expanding into other categories like chess.

Duolingo offers its full language courses for free, monetizing through it with a combination of advertising and a premium subscription tier that removes ads and adds features. This approach has been essential for Duolingo to achieve massive scale. According to Statista, Duolingo has by far been the most downloaded language learning app in 2024.

I believe with such a significant lead, they have by far the most resources to continuously improve their app, especially now with the use of LLMs. Thus, I believe it is worth further examining the company.

Total Addressable Market

The market opportunity for Duolingo is quite large, and it is expanding. According to the Grandviewresearch the global language learning market is projected to more than double to $54 billion with an annual CAGR of 16.6%. I believe Duolingo is, with its large user base, perfectly positioned to take advantage of this trend. In my opinion, the company’s revenues should grow at least at the same rate as the market, but I would argue that the growth rate will be faster due to Duolingo disrupting private tutors and getting into new categories like chess. I strongly believe that with the use of AI, Duolingo will be able to create more personalized lessons similar to what you would get with a regular tutor. They are already doing this in their Duolingo Max tier. With time, their offerings should only get better.

User Growth

Duolingo managed to reach 47.7 million DAU in Q2 2025 (Chart 1), which represented 40% YoY growth. This is significantly above the aforementioned expected CAGR of the language learning market, which clearly means Duolingo is taking market share from private tutors. Paid subscribers also reached an all-time high of 10.9 million members, a 37% increase from the prior year.

Once Duolingo gains DAUs, the company should try to convert them to subscribers. Currently, the rate of subscribers to DAUs is holding steady at around 23% - Chart 2. I actually expect this percentage to slowly get higher with the increased use of AI, for reasons I will discuss in the AI disruption section. Note that subscription revenue accounted for approximately 83% of total revenue in Q2 2025, indicating that conversion to subscribers is vital for Duolingo's continued success.

I should also point out that Duolingo offers Super, Max, and Family Plans, with Max being, in my opinion, the most important one as I will discuss later. Super offers removal of ads and the family plan allows up to six users to share the benefits of a Super Duolingo subscription under a single, discounted plan.

AI disruption

Duolingo's stock recently fell sharply due to fears that LLMs will disrupt Duolingo’s business. I disagree, and I actually believe that Duolingo, with the use of AI, will disrupt private tutors.

For example, traditional private tutors offer personalized, one-on-one lessons, but they can be quite expensive, from $30 to $80 per hour, based on my research, though it also depends on the region. In contrast, Duolingo Max costs $30 per month, and you can use it as much as you want, 24/7. For those unfamiliar with Max, it offers user, for example, the ability to video chat with AI character Lilly and talk about different topics with her. AI will then correct you in real time should you make a mistake. Other features are, in my opinion, less important compared to the potential of Lilly. I strongly believe that the more users Duolingo has, the more sophisticated the AI will become, due to larger datasets and better fine-tuning. Duolingo should become even more tutor-like and personalized, and eventually, I see no reason that it can’t be as good as a private tutor.

Duolingo’s CEO, Luis von Ahn, said in a recent interview that “AI’s ability to individualize learning will lead to most teaching being done by computers in the next few decades.” I couldn’t agree more. I do believe Duolingo has a bright future, especially because of AI, and has a good chance to become a “pocket tutor.”

Moat

Data

There is a commonly agreed-upon fact that the one who has the most data will be able to train the best AI. Currently, as I discussed in the introduction, Duolingo has by far the most downloads compared to any other language learning app; thus, they have by far the largest user base. This user base will be the reason that Duolingo will be able to train the best AI pocket tutor. In a post from 2022, Duolingo stated they get 200,000 user reports per day. I believe now, with a much higher user count, that number should be much higher. I would argue that this is a significant moat that the company has, and it will be able to improve its app much faster than anyone else.

Additionally, I believe Lilly (AI video chatbot) will also be able to learn from talking and interacting with many different users and, in time, learn from them and become better much faster due to the large amount of data that no competitor has.

Brand

Besides data and tech superiority, Duolingo has one of the most iconic and recognizable brands in the language learning market. Everyone who wants to learn languages online knows Duolingo, which is the reason why it is by far the most downloaded language-learning app. I believe as long as Duolingo doesn’t fall behind, and due to the largest datasets and AI integration, I do not think it will, it should remain in the lead.

Competition and risks

I believe the highest risk to Duolingo is the competition. As I discussed in the AI disruption part, I believe AI will be a tailwind for Duolingo and not a headwind, as many fear. I believe this narrative is quite similar to how investors were afraid Google (GOOGL) would lose search leadership, but in the end that didn’t happen, and Google’s stock performed quite well in 2025.

So let's look into the competition. According to Statista four apps are at the top of language learning: Duolingo with 500M+ downloads, Babbel and Buddy.ai with both over 50M downloads and Lingutown with 10M+ downloads. Duolingo has the highest rating of all the apps mentioned, with a stellar 4.7/5 rating. This gives me confidence that Duolingo really has the advantage over the competition. I firmly believe that due to its large data sets and AI, the gap between Duolingo and the competition will only widen.

Valuation

I expect that Duolingo’s revenues will grow above the industry-expected CAGR of around 17%, due to the reasons stated in the article. Analysts expect revenues to grow 36% and 25% in 2025 and 2026, respectively. After that, it would be reasonable to assume Duolingo would grow somewhere around 20-25%, depending on how bullish the investor is. In the bull case, I used growth rates a bit higher than analysts’ projections due to Duolingo constantly beating analysts’ expectations.

Duolingo had a 12% net margin in 2024 and a TTM net margin of 13%. Margin has been steadily and significantly improving. Due to user conversion to subscribers, I believe Duolingo will, in the future, become more like an SaaS company with high margins and premium valuation. Net margin should also significantly improve due to the nature of software companies. For example, Adobe (ADBE) has a net margin of 30%. In the bull case, I expect something similar for Duolingo, and in the base case, I expect around a 24% net margin, something similar to what Zoom (ZM) was able to achieve despite all the competition.

I believe there is a higher chance for the bull case scenario, due to Duolingo's ongoing AI innovations and the potential to disrupt the traditional tutoring market. Still, I think both scenarios offer significant upside, with 77% return in the base case and 151% return in the bull case.

Plan

I view the recent pullback as an opportunity, as it presents a good entry price for initiating a position.

data.title: Investing Implications As U.S. Government Enters Shutdown
data.description:
With a U.S. government shutdown underway, what are the implications for investors? Jeff Evans, VP, Director and Lead of Empirical Research and PM Support with TD Asset Management joins MoneyTalk to discuss.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Investors have another risk to consider with a U.S. government shutdown underway. Joining us now to discuss what it could mean for the markets, Jeff Evans, VP, director and lead for empirical research and PM support with TD Asset Management.

Jeff, always great to have you on the program.

Jeff Evans: Great to be here. Thanks for having me.

Greg Bonnell: Markets are fluid, obviously. We don't know what they're going to do from one minute to the next, but it seemed heading into this we did see what we'd expect in perhaps gold and bonds and in equities. But when we think about a government shutdown and the mechanics of it, what does it actually mean for the markets longer term?

Jeff Evans: Yeah, so it certainly hasn't been a dull year in Washington. Lots going on. But I think what's important for investors to keep in mind on a government shutdown, typically these are short-term events, last for a couple of days. Most government shutdowns are less than a week. And the main reason for that is that these are very expensive.

Now, obviously, for the federal government, not so much. They're not spending any money over the next little while while they're shut. But when you think about all the knock-on effects from that, all of the businesses, all of the consumers that are either directly exposed to this-- 700,000 federal workers potentially furloughed-- and then all the indirect impacts on other businesses-- services, spending, and so on. When we've talked to economists about this, the typical impact would be about 0.25 to 0.5% of GDP per month of shutdown.

So it's not big in the context of the U.S. economy, but if you think about it in terms of economic growth, the U.S. economy usually only grows 3-4% a year. Half a percentage point is potentially a fairly big hit to growth. So I think that's the way we're thinking about it. It is a much bigger impact on the growth side of things.

And one of the things that makes this shutdown potentially a little bit more interesting, or concerning, is most shutdowns are less than seven days. But if you look at the last shutdown in 2018, that was actually the longest on record at 35 days-- also under the Trump administration and in a period where we had very strong partisan politics. Nobody wanted to get together or compromise. And what ultimately led to that was you had a number of federal workers that refused to actually come back and perform essential services. LaGuardia Airport, for example, I think was shut down at one point. And that's what kind of forced lawmakers to come back and sort things out.

The other dynamic here as well is there's a lot of posturing going into the midterm elections for next year. Lots of politics behind the scenes to figure out who could be blamed for the shutdown. And so, you're already starting to hear from some of the senior leaders, the Democrats and Republicans, that this could stretch out even longer than 2018. And I think that's maybe where there's a little bit of concern.

The other thing to keep in mind in the shutdown in 2018, it was only a partial shutdown. The government actually had funding for about half of the departments. So half of the government stayed open, half shut. In this shutdown, none of those funding bills have been passed. It would be a full shutdown. And when we think about the potential impact, it gets you probably closer to that 0.5% of GDP. Again, we don't really know exactly what it would be, but probably a bigger impact than what you saw in 2018.

As far as the impact for overall markets, when we've looked at this over time, usually the equity markets look through these sorts of events-- again, because typically it's a short-term event, less than a week. Even if it does stretch out to a month and it's a half a point of GDP, in the context of the next 100 years of U.S. economic performance and corporate earnings, it's really a drop in the bucket. So equity markets tend not to react too much to this. And in fact, in the 2018 period, markets sold off into the news. And then as soon as the shutdown happened, equity started to rally. It's kind of similar to what we've seen this morning-- it started negative and then back in the green fairly quickly.

I think the bigger concern here is we're not going to get economic data. It may not be just the jobs report. It could be inflation. It could be news about jobs over time. And that has the potential to impact Federal Reserve decision-making. If they were to put their cutting campaign on pause, that's when you might see a bigger equity market reaction.

So, I think what we'd be watching here is the tone from Federal Reserve officials over the next couple of weeks. And I mean, the other piece of this, too, is they're also going to be taking into account that 700,000 federal workers are going to be furloughed. That probably shouldn't cause them to pause their cutting cycle, but I think that's the key thing to focus on. It's really probably the Fed and Fed policy that has the potentially biggest impact here over the next few weeks.

Greg Bonnell: Lots of moving pieces there, and obviously we need to keep a careful eye on the developments. Other pockets of the market, obviously, real estate and infrastructure-- when we have you on, we talk about that quite a bit. The real estate outlook-- are some of the parts of the sector starting to pick up?

Jeff Evans: So it has not been a great five years for real estate, and the main reason for that has been, obviously, a 40-year increase in inflation-- a very significant inflation increase that also led to the biggest and fastest increase in interest rates in 40 years. For a leveraged sector like real estate, that was a pretty significant headwind over the last few years.

But the good news is we're now mostly through the end of that adjustment process. A lot of the REITs that issued cheap debt in the pandemic, that's starting to roll over at higher levels today. What that's meant is a lot of the rent growth that REITs have achieved over the past few years, it's basically just going to the banks to pay higher interest expense. But as that debt reprices-- and we've got maybe a little bit more in '26 and '27, but the bulk of it is done-- that should allow more rent growth to move to the bottom line and ultimately support higher growth from real estate over time.

What we're also starting to see is, we're seeing reaction in asset values. So we're starting to see more transactions in the market. Maybe a little bit of a pause at the start of the year with tariffs, but certainly over the summer we're hearing more and more leasing activity-- even in office, surprisingly, given some of the concerns over AI. We're seeing lots of office activity in places like New York and San Francisco.

Broadly speaking, real estate transaction market activity is picking up. And that also tends to produce a more positive view of the sector going forward. When we talk to some of the bigger players as well-- the Brookfields, the Blackstones, the BlackRocks-- that have a significant private market exposure, all of them are starting to make moves. We've seen a couple of fairly large deals recently, including a warehouse deal in Europe just this week of a couple billion dollars. So you're seeing the bigger players also becoming a lot more active.

Really, the key thing for us, occupancy has actually stayed pretty stable over the last couple of years, and that's really the core driver of real estate performance. If there's demand and tenants want to be in the buildings, that's the key thing. There's a few moving parts in certain sectors, but broadly, occupancy has stayed stable.

The key is rent growth. With all of the economic pressures, it's been harder to push rents-- sometimes because of interest rates, sometimes because of inflation. There's also been a lot of extra supply that's come into the market, particularly in residential and industrial. Taking a little bit of time to work through that.

But as we look into 2027, that's when a lot of that supply disappears. We should start to see a more favourable interest rate backdrop. Ultimately, that becomes a much more landlord-friendly market as you get out. So 2026 may be still a bit of a transition year, but the current markets, of course, will look through that. They'll start looking into '27, '28. And we're becoming more constructive as we get closer to that, then we can start to see some acceleration in rent growth and overall cash flow growth from the REIT sector.

Greg Bonnell: Alright, very interesting stuff there on the real estate front. What about the infrastructure space? What are we thinking here?

Jeff Evans: So, there are a number of different moving parts there. Maybe start with utilities. The big story on the utility side continues to be, of course, data centres. We continue to see an enormous amount of demand for data centres. And the significant announcement a few weeks ago with Oracle (ORCL) and OpenAI, this just continues in a pretty significant way. We're not seeing any signs of hyperscalers pulling back their capex. All of that leads to significant electricity demand over the next several years. And this data centre, everybody's talking about it today, but it's really not going to happen until '27, '28, '29. It's going to take a long time to get the infrastructure in place. And that's when you start to see the pickup in power demand.

The other big development is the One Big Beautiful Bill Act that came out over the summer. Bonus depreciation is going to be a huge driver for investment into next year. Still a little uncertainty on how the Treasury is going to apply certain rules, so companies are still waiting to really get aggressive. But as you get into '26, we should see more construction of warehouse manufacturing facilities. And ultimately, all of those need electricity to function, same way as data centres. What that means for utilities, 5-8% or 6-8% earnings growth is what most utilities are guiding for. Maybe a little bit closer to the high end of the range, given how much is going on in the U.S. And because of just the number of different demand drivers we're seeing, it's not just one or two years of EPS growth, it's potentially 5 to 10. So it's creating a very, very visible pipeline of growth for utilities. We remain pretty constructive there.

And then the pipeline side, maybe that's the other flip side of this. You mentioned earlier oil prices being a little bit weaker. What you tend to see in that environment is a relative trade where the Canadian pipes, Enbridge (ENB) and TransCanada (TRP), tend to outperform when commodity prices are weaker relative to the U.S. pipelines. That's generally what we've seen over the course of the year. So, on a relative basis, stronger performance from the Canadians.

In the U.S., one interesting thing that we've seen this morning-- Targa (TRGP), which is a pipeline out of the Permian Basin, announced a new pipeline coming into service in '27. That's interesting because there had been some concerns about Permian growth slowing down. Maybe lower volumes coming out of that basin. This is, I think, a vote of confidence in the long-term prospects for that. But what we're broadly seeing in the U.S. is, it's a focus on gas pipelines, which brings you right back to the data centre thematic. So it's companies like Williams (WMB) or DT Midstream (DTM) that have a very significant focus or platform near where data centres are. I think that's what we've generally seen within the pipeline space: a preference for Canadian names if oil remains relatively weak, and then a preference for gas names is where we see the greatest opportunities in that area.

Original Post

data.title: Nike's Transformation Strategy Is Finally Delivering Results
data.description:
Investment Thesis

In my previous coverage of NIKE (NYSE:NKE), I maintained a BUY rating, based on early signs of a turnaround driven by the company’s Win Now strategy and renewed focus on innovation as key levers to accelerate revenue growth.

This article provides an updated view on the company’s potential future performance following its Q1’26 report and highlights the evidence that NKE’s turnaround has already begun, supported by management’s continued strategic actions.

Despite headwinds from tariffs and softness in Greater China, NIKE is actively shaping its future by doubling down on what it knows best: the performance and sportswear industry.

Let me explain!

Q1’26 Results as Evidence of Turnaround

Let’s start with what most investors focus on: revenue. NIKE’s revenue grew by just 1% YoY, reaching $11.7 billion in Q1’26.

The most important factor, in my view, is that North America -NIKE’s largest market-reported a 4% increase in Q1. Some investors might argue that 4% growth is not impressive, but this is the first time since Q1’24 that NIKE has posted positive revenue growth in North America. In other words, after nearly two years, NIKE is finally growing again in its core region, which accounts for almost 43% of total revenue.

A similar pattern is seen in Europe, Middle East & Africa (EMEA), which delivered 6% revenue growth -NIKE’s second largest market, representing about 28.5% of total revenue- while Asia Pacific & Latin America (APLA) posted a modest 1.9% increase.

NIKE reported impressive yoy revenue growth in the Apparel segment, reversing the revenue declines seen over the past two years, along with solid yoy growth in the Equipment segment.

Its main segment, Footwear, continues to decline, but the rate of decline has slowed significantly. From the double-digit revenue drop that started in Q1’25, NIKE reported just a 0.7% revenue decline in Q1’26, signaling that the break-even point for the footwear business may not be far away.

A major factor behind this improvement—and, in my view, a key driver for potential revenue growth in the coming quarters - is the 20% growth in the running business. Even more promising is the fact that Running was the first business to implement the new Sport Offense strategy, which aims to accelerate innovation and consumer connection.

As CEO, President & Director, Elliott Hill, stated:

Our Running business gives us an early window into the kind of impact we expect out of the Sport Offense. Our Running team moved fastest into our new formation and was the first to get sharper on the insights of their athletes.

After Win Now strategy, Sport Offense Is Here

I have already discussed the benefits of the Win Now strategy and why I believe it could significantly boost the company’s revenue. In Q1’26 report, top management introduced Sport Offense- a new organizational model with small, cross-functional teams by sport and brand, designed to enable faster market response and capture consumer trends more effectively.

As mentioned earlier, Running was the first category to implement Sport Offense, delivering an impressive 20% YoY growth in Q1’26. Under this model, each team includes product creation, brand marketing, and marketplace execution for a specific sport.

Elliott Hill has pointed out that Nike needs to rediscover its sport-driven identity and position itself to consumers as a premium sportswear company, specifically stated:

And again, there's no question in my mind that putting sport and the athlete back at the center of everything that we do, puts us back on offense.

I also see Sport Offense as complementary to the company’s Geo Express Lane, which supports local-for-local production. What does this mean? Products are designed and manufactured locally (e.g., in China) to meet local consumer preferences, reducing the time from design to shelf and enabling Nike to respond faster to regional trends.

Win Now and Sport Offense, together with Geo Express Lane, are the core pillars of NIKE’s future growth strategy. One more piece of evidence that these pillars are driving the turnaround is that Q1 EPS of $0.49 beats estimates by $0.22 and revenue of $11.7B beats estimates by $710M.

Valuation and Risk Analysis

The company’s PE and EV/EBIT ratios are very close to their historical levels, supporting my view that investors cannot yet see the full potential of the company’s growth.

Additionally, fwd PE ratios of 29.91, 24.28 and 19.12 for 2027, 2028 and 2029 respectively, signal that NKE’s current share price does not reflect its future growth, as these ratios are significantly lower than the historical average PE ratio of 35. As already mentioned in my previous reports, Nike is close to finding its bottom in revenue decline during FY25; afterwards, analysts expect significant revenue growth.

Therefore, I feel confident to say that NKE’s current stock price is undervalued, considering the beginning of its turnaround story with actual evidence and the anticipated revenue growth.

However, as a company operating in the retail industry, NKE faces significant risks that it has to manage. One of them is the continued revenue decline in the China market. The Geo Express Lane, mentioned above, will be mainly applied in the region of China in order to boost revenue. In Q1’26, NKE’s revenue in this region was down 9% YoY. While the company may see a long-term opportunity there, the Geo Express Lane has not yet proved to be fully effective. Therefore, a positive revenue trend in China is a major aspect that could benefit the company and support its performance, while a continued decline could drive a stock price correction.

I do not see NIKE Direct revenues YoY decline of 4% — driven by a 12% decrease in NIKE Brand Digital and a 1% decrease in NIKE-owned retail stores — as a major risk. The reason lies in the statement from the company’s Executive VP & CFO, Matthew Friend:

We've reduced promo days, we've improved the markdown rates. We've reduced the classic share of business. We've reduced the launch share of business and we pulled back on paid media as it was largely driving bottom of funnel traffic to our platforms.

In my view, this move is a strategic action to rebuild NIKE’s positioning as a premium sportswear brand and regain customer loyalty — a factor that could support higher profit margins in the near future.

However, I need to highlight the risk of tariffs, which now represent an impact of approximately $1.5B, up from the $1B in the previous quarter. While this additional cost has not significantly affected the company’s strong cash position, it does weigh on profitability. Of course, tariffs are a headwind across the entire industry, not just for Nike. Still, given that the company is currently in a transformation process, it remains more sensitive to macroeconomic volatilities and geopolitical risks.

These pressures are already reflected in the reduction of gross margin by 320 basis points in Q1’26d, down to 42.2%, compared to 45.4% in Q1’25, as well as in the 31% yoy decline in net income, falling to $727M in Q1’26 from $1,051M in Q1’25.

Conclusion

As already emphasized in my previous articles, NKE is in a turnaround phase. Evidence of this turnaround and the effectiveness of the Win Now strategy is reflected in revenue growth across key regional markets, North America and EMEA.

The new operational model Sport Offense has already boosted Running business sales, while the Geo Express Lane model - which mainly targets the China region - remains the key bet on whether NKE can reignite growth in this strategically important market with massive long-term potential.

data.title: BST: Still Trades At A Discount To NAV, Positioned To Benefit From Lower Rates
data.description:
At the September FOMC meeting, the Fed finally cut rates, which is bullish for the economy and especially small caps, yet the BlackRock Science and Technology Trust (NYSE:BST) is still trading at a -5.07% discount to net asset value (NAV). BST may not be trading at the levels it was during 2021, when growth was all investors cared about, but it has appreciated by just over 48% since its December 2022 lows. Over the past year, shares of BST have climbed 18.72% despite dipping below $30 during the tariff uncertainty. The reality is that investors are able to add shares of BST at a -5.07% discount to its NAV and collect a 7.02% distribution yield in an environment where the Fed is cutting rates and the risk-free rate of return is declining. I think that BST is going to be a direct beneficiary of a lower rate environment, as its distribution has established a long track record of generating recurring income and its underlying companies benefit from relief from higher rates. BST has also rebalanced its top holdings as they added Oracle (ORCL) and a private company, Anduril, which I am a huge fan of. I think BST is positioned to directly benefit from the companies that are shaping the A.I. landscape while capitalizing on trends that will be unlocked within biotech from the advancements in A.I. As we head into Q3 earnings, I think BST will continue to appreciate in value and finish the year higher than it is today.

Following up on my previous article about BST

The market has been doing well since my last article about BST (can be read here), as the S&P 500 is up 11.01%. Since then, shares of BST have climbed 10.83%, and after the monthly distributions are factored in, the total return is 13.71%. BST was trading at a -4.59% discount to its NAV while having a distribution yield that exceeded 8%, which I felt was an opportunity for shareholders. I was expecting the Fed to lower rates, which would cause the cost of capital to decline for its underlying holdings. The public holdings in BST’s top-10 holdings had an average of 42.50% EPS growth over the next 2 years which I felt was bullish. I am following up with a new article to discuss why I am very bullish about BST after the recent rate cut and how I feel their portfolio is positioned to benefit from a lower rate environment. I think BST is still a strong candidate to generate a large amount of income at a time when it will be less lucrative to remain on the sidelines with capital appreciation from the same investment.

BST’s share price hasn’t appreciated at the same rate as its net asset value which is bullish for shareholders

The last time that I evaluated BST, it was trading at $37.43 with a NAV of $39.15, which was a separation of 4.59% between where shares of BST were trading and the actual value of its book of assets. While shares of BST have appreciated by 10.83% since then, its book of assets has increased by 11.88% as they are now worth $43.80 per share. The fact that both the share price and the NAV is increasing but there is a separation regarding the rate at which they are appreciating is interesting to me. BST is now trading at a -5.07% discount to NAV despite shares trading for over $40. Theoretically, investors are getting a better deal today than they were previously, as we’re able to buy the book of assets at a steeper discount. I think this is bullish for shareholders because there is the possibility that BST can close the gap and trade at a 1:1 level with it’s NAV in the future. The other interesting aspect is that if M&A activity heats up we could see the IPO market come back to life, and BST has Anduril and Databricks Series F shares in their top-10 holdings. If either company goes public for significantly more than what it's worth on the private market, it could help boost both the NAV and share price for BST.

BST has repositioned its portfolio a bit and is more geared towards technology. When I look at the public companies in its holdings, I see a tremendous amount of growth and upside potential. The combination of these 8 companies is trading at 58.02 times 2025 earnings and 34.68 times 2027 earnings, which represents 53.81% of EPS growth over the next 2-years. If I remove Snowflake (SNOW), the remaining 7 public companies trade for 25.37 times 2027 earnings with 46.85% of growth over the next 2 years. I am very excited because the majority of these companies are trading for attractive forward valuations in a market that is making all-time highs. These are the companies that are likely to lead the market higher and beat their top and bottom lines.

Some of these analyst estimates could be light, in my opinion, as the CapEx war is only going to increase. The combination of Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), and Meta Platforms (META) allocated $88.25 billion toward CapEx in Q2 2025. This was an increase of 66.97% ($35.39 billion) YoY. The growth rate that these companies are spending to build out data centers is expanding, considering that in Q2 2024 the combination of these 4 companies increased their CapEx spend QoQ by 19.33% ($8.56 billion) to $52.85 billion. In Q2 2025, their spending increased 22.73% ($16.34 billion) to $88.25 billion. While the trend is that CapEx allocation is accelerating, the reason why I think it will continue to expand is because of the increased cash from operations being generated. In 2021 these 4 hyperscalers generated $272.4 billion in cash from operations. Over the next 3 ½ years, their combined cash from operations increased by 81.09% to $493.31 billion. This allowed them to increase their CapEx allocation by 133.07% from $125 billion to $291.35 billion. As their CapEx spend increased by 133.07%, the amount of free cash flow [FCF] they produced increased by 37.02% to $201.96 billion. There is no shortage of profitability, and these companies have taken a blank check approach to A.I. innovation. If these trends continue companies like NVDA and Broadcom (AVGO) could see increased EPS potential, and AMZN, META, GOOGL, and MSFT could monetize this innovation more than what is expected.

The lower interest rate environment is bullish for BST

BST is currently paying a $3 per share distribution, which works out to a 7.22% yield. Since inception, BST has generated $26.61 in distribution income as their initial monthly distribution of $0.10 increased by 150% to $0.25 since 2014. Long-term investors were able to pay $20 for shares of BST at inception and generate 133.03% on their capital from the distributions. Investors also got an additional $21.66 in capital appreciation, which was a gain of 108.30%. BST has never lowered its distribution and has paid several special distributions over the years. I believe that the Fed starting to cut rates is bullish because now the 3-month to 50-year bonds are all under a 4% yield. This makes BST’s 7.22% distribution yield look even more attractive. There is still over $7.48 trillion sitting in money markets, and the interest being generated is only going to decline and some of that capital could find its way back into the equity markets. I believe that BST will become increasingly popular because of its distribution attributes and how it compares to the risk-free rate of return.

In addition to the distribution, I believe that BST’s underlying holdings will benefit a great deal from lower interest rates. BST invests in science and technology companies which are focused on growth and are valued on future earnings potential rather than current cash flow. A lower rate environment will reduce the discount rate applied to their profitability and could expand their earnings multiples. If this occurs, it would boost the NAV of BST and likely cause the share price to go higher. BST also uses leverage, and lower interest rates will reduce their borrowing rates. This could cause their margins to expand and allow BST’s leverage to work more effectively in boosting yields and NAV growth. I think that we could also see investors leave fixed assets in search of growth as we could get a risk on environment. If that occurs, the underlying assets in BST’s portfolio should do well and cause BST to continue appreciating in value.

Conclusion

When I look back at BST, I have been bullish since the summer of 2022, and its total return hasn’t disappointed me. I am not expecting BST to exceed the returns of the market but I do believe the market will go higher and take shares of BST along for the ride. Currently investors can get more than a -5% discount on BST’s book of assets, including exposure to private companies such as Anduril and Databricks. BST has the potential to continue generating capital appreciation for its shareholders while presenting an opportunity to lock in a 7.22% yield on cost from the distribution. I’m pretty excited about BST even at these levels, as investors are getting a strong book of assets at a discount with recurring monthly income that exceeds the risk-free rate of return.

data.title: Synchrony Financial: Even After The Recent Gain, I Still See Value In This Stock
data.description:
Over the last few months, Synchrony Financial (NYSE:SYF) has delivered quite good returns to its investors, with its stock price growing over 35% in the last 6 months, primarily driven by the vacation of the cap on the credit card late fee rate in April 2025 and a better-than-expected credit quality environment.

While the stock price might look a little stretched now, I believe its valuations are not near being expensive and warrant a buy rating. The positive trend on the asset quality front as well as potential NIM expansion from Fed fund rate cuts further boosts SYF's prospects.

Financials Are Strong, Barring Growth

From 2023, SYF's management started to proactively prepare for a potential rate cap on credit card late fees. The company did some product pricing and policy changes (PPPC) in order to make up for the losses that the company would eventually witness post enactment of this ruling. In April this year, the final rule on the credit card late fees was vacated, which means SYF would not need to bear the losses from these rulings. On the other hand, the company reaped the benefits of the proactive steps that they took to mitigate these potential losses, leading to an additional other income of $61 million in 1Q25. Management further said that they do not intend to roll back these measures, which means this additional income will be sustainable.

In addition to the benefit from the proactive price adjustments, the asset quality was another count where investors were delighted. Over the last few quarters, the 30- and 90-day+ delinquency ratios, as well as the net charge-off ratio, have cooled off significantly; even on a YoY basis, the improvement is fairly visible.

Now, management has cited their stricter lending policies as being at play in managing these credit quality ratios; one could also give credit to the better-than-expected macroeconomic environment for this improvement. The overall asset quality across the board in the USA, especially for the credit card customers, has improved considerably over the last 6-9 months as the delinquency ratios declined after a period of uptick from 2022-2024. Moreover, many also speculated that the delinquency ratio might surge again after the tariff saga, but so far no material deterioration is visible.

Now with the recent government shutdown, many are speculating that this might lead to a wave of delinquencies across the economy, impacting the higher-risk segments like credit cards. As a result, many card issuers, like SYF and others, have witnessed a brief cut in their stock price.

I believe the risk of a rise in delinquencies to be meager, first of all, if we look at the history, government shutdowns have not lasted long, with the longest one in 2018 lasting for 35 days. Second, furloughed employees are paid back in full when they return to work, so they would pay back their obligations once they receive back pay from the government.

However, if the shutdown lasts longer than expected (2 months or more), it should have a second- and higher-order effect on the economy, as the government is one of the largest spenders in the economy, and a halt in spending from the government could have a multiplier effect on spending slowdown. While this is a possibility, I believe the chances of a long shutdown are quite grim, as this could have major political damage for the ruling party.

Another concern regarding the shutdown is that President Donald Trump has threatened to lay off many federal workers after the shutdown. Again, I believe this to be a bargaining tactic; even if it happens, I believe it would be a minor layoff, as large-scale layoffs would be politically damaging and operationally challenging.

Therefore, I believe delinquencies should remain stable, and SYF should continue to build upon the momentum of recent quarters and post better ratios, i.e., move forward or at least remain stable.

Additionally, I also believe that SYF should also benefit from expanding NIMs. SYF's balance sheet is generally liability sensitive, i.e., in a rate cut cycle, SYF tends to witness a near-term NIMs pressure as the yields tend to reprice while liabilities take a while to reprice. While the headwind with respect to the yield was negated from the PPPC initiatives, the full benefit from the lower cost of funds should start following in the latter half of the year when more of the CD fundings get repriced at lower rates.

While SYF is delivering on most of the metrics, its asset growth of late has been underwhelming. The company has delivered negative single-digit YoY volume growth for 5 out of the last six quarters; meanwhile, loan receivables have also started to tick into the negative territory for the last two quarters. I believe there are two reasons for this: first, the high-interest rate, which has led many consumers to hold on to their discretionary spending as well as cut back on less discretionary spending. And second, management's deliberate decision to tighten lending standards to curb the asset quality risk.

On the interest rate front, the pressure seems to be easing; however, its benefit still remains to be seen. On the lending standard front, management has stated they will loosen some of the lending restrictions in order to boost growth, but I believe one should not expect much of a boost from here, as management won't loosen the standards much, as it might affect asset quality.

Additionally, the company is also partnering with various players like Amazon Pay, PayPal physical card, and Walmart/OnePay. These partnerships can drive meaningful growth for SYF in its digital segment for the medium to long term.

Overall, SYF's prospects look good to me with stable asset quality and NIM expansion possibility. While growth is a pain point as of now, management is taking necessary measures to remedy it. I believe SYF's management should maintain a delicate balance between growth and asset quality. Even if they remain a little cautious for the time being, I believe the growth will sooner or later get back on track due to these large partnerships and delayed benefit from rate cuts.

Valuation

SYF currently trades at 1.52x forward price-to-book ratio, which is higher than its own 5-year average of 1.37x. If we look at SYF's ROE, the ratio stands at ~21.3%, slightly lower than its 5-year average of 22.3%. So based on the P/B ratio of ROE, we get to a yield (ROE/price-to-book) of 14% as compared to the 5-year yield of 16.2%.

So comparing the valuation on a historical basis does point toward a slightly stretched valuation for SYF. But I believe there is a decent headroom with respect to the ROE numbers, as the company would start lapping in benefits from higher NIMs going forward. Just to put that into perspective, a 100 bps improvement in NIMs should lead to an 8 percentage point better ROE (assuming 15% higher average equity will lead to a 4 percentage point better ROE).

As per management, they expect ROE to improve to 16%, similar to our assumed NIM. Based on these ROEs, the current book value ratio will result in a yield of 17%-19%, which is quite lucrative.

Risk

The primary risk regarding SYF or any other credit card lender is macroeconomic risk. In times of macroeconomic stress, the first wave of delinquencies is witnessed by the credit card lenders, and the delinquency rate tends to rise quite rapidly for credit card lenders in these times. However, given the current stable delinquency rate across the industry as well as in SYF's balance sheet, I believe this is not much of a risk as of now.

The government shutdown could also be categorized as a risk, but I don't believe it would be significant enough to trigger a major delinquency risk or macroeconomic risk, as the administration would not want to weather the political backlash arising from it.

Conclusion

Despite growth concerns, which should resolve going forward, I rate SYF a buy given its stable asset quality, NIM expansion prospects, and lucrative valuation.

data.title: Don't Be Misled By This Drop In French Industrial Production
data.description:
By Charlotte de Montpellier

A misleading decline

Manufacturing output in France fell by 0.7% month-on-month in August, according to the latest data from Insee. While this drop may raise concerns, it should be viewed in context. July’s figures were revised upward, from an initially reported -1.7% to -0.5%, and overall activity remains above May levels.

Much of the recent volatility stems from the aerospace and space sectors. After a sharp surge in June (with 'other transport equipment' production jumping 17.3% month-on-month), production normalised in July and August. This swing has mechanically dragged down headline figures following June’s spike.

Fragile growth

Despite recent fluctuations, French industry is trending higher than earlier in the year, up 1.1% compared to the average of the first five months. This offers a modestly positive signal amid a subdued global backdrop. Still, the outlook remains mixed. While GDP is likely to have expanded in the third quarter of 2025, risks are mounting for the year-end.

Domestic demand remains anaemic. Household spending on goods was basically flat in August, edging up just 0.1% after a sharper-than-expected 0.6% decline in July. This lethargy persists despite low inflation (1.2% in September) and relatively strong purchasing power gains compared to other European economies. Political uncertainty, fears of tax hikes, and concerns over unemployment are eroding consumer confidence, prompting households to save more. The savings rate hit a record 18.9% in 2Q, and we expect it to remain elevated, further dampening consumption and undermining hopes for a near-term rebound.

Budget complications ahead

Even if industrial production holds up, growth may stagnate in the fourth quarter, leaving France on a weak footing heading into 2026. This complicates the fiscal outlook. Prime Minister Sébastien Lecornu recently acknowledged the deteriorating environment, revising the 2026 growth forecast down to 1% from 1.2%. That may still be too optimistic. We expect just 0.8% growth in 2026, following 0.6% in 2025. Under these conditions, passing a fully-fledged budget in Parliament this year looks increasingly unlikely, and political and economic uncertainty may persist well into next year.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Rates Spark: All Major Dutch Funds Now On Track For 2026
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker

Bearish momentum in euro rates may need to wait for more clarity from the US

We continue to see 2.8% as the next target for the 10Y euro swap rate to aim for, but we may have to wait for more clarity on US rates first. The 10Y rate was already close to that level earlier this year after the German spending announcement. 'Liberation Day' pushed rates down again, but recovering growth should keep the bearish momentum going from here.

Whilst the correlation between US rates and euro rates has decreased significantly since Trump's election, a divergence in different directions remains difficult. From a structural view, we see the 10Y UST yield rising from here, towards 4.5%, but the government shutdown and some disappointing jobs market data may delay that move in the near term. As such, until we get more clarity there, the 10Y euro swap may be stuck closer to 2.7%.

More than €400bn pension assets on track to transition in 2026

PMT (c.€85bn AuM), the third-largest Dutch pension fund, confirmed its ambition to transition on 1 January 2026. While no official approval from the Dutch regulator (DNB) is mentioned, the announcement does suggest the fund is on track. The 10s30s curve briefly steepened by 2bp on the news, suggesting such headlines are closely watched by markets. With Bouw already having approval, and PFZW also being on track, together these three funds make up €400bn in assets. This means we are increasingly confident about significant flows out of longer-dated swaps and bonds we can expect in 2026.

PMT previously shared plans to unwind its excessive interest rate hedges over a period of six months, reducing the immediate market impact. Rather than attempting to rebalance the portfolio immediately on 2 January 2026, the fund intends to reduce the hedging ratio in steps, which will be dynamically determined depending on market conditions. Other funds have not been as explicit about their strategy, also to prevent speculators from taking advantage. We still believe funds will try to unwind their excessive hedges as soon as possible to prevent rates from rising against them. But as PMT’s approach also demonstrates, we don’t expect a disorderly sell-off.

Friday’s events and market views

With the US official jobs report delayed due to the shutdown, the focus will be on ISM services. It is expected to weaken somewhat to 51 from 52. The other focus will be Fed speakers, with Williams, Logan and Jefferson scheduled to speak.

In EUR rates, we will look at the final services PMIs and also a slate of central bank speakers at a farewell symposium for Klaas Knot. They include, among others, Lagarde, Schnabel and Knot’s successor Sleijpen, as well as the Bank of England's Bailey.

After market close, Moody’s is scheduled to review its rating of the EU (Aaa/Stable).

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Freedom Holding: The Stock That Proves Emerging Markets Still Deliver
data.description:
My Thesis Update

Since March 2021, when I first initiated my "Buy" investment rating on Freedom Holding Corp. (NASDAQ:FRHC) here on Seeking Alpha, the stock has been up by 228%, beating the S&P 500 index's (SPY) gain of 73% for the same period. I was updating my coverage regularly with the same bullish rating, and I'm happy to see that my calls aged well (even the most recent ones):

The outperformance of FRHC has been great, and I still think it's there to stay because Freedom Holding keeps entering new niches within its main geographical market, gradually building its superapp with banking, insurance, brokerage, telecom, e-commerce, and lifestyle services. It's not a conventional financial holding anymore - Freedom has become a regional digital ecosystem builder, which I think will be further scaled beyond just Kazakhstan. We're already seeing the company's attempts to explore other Central Asian telecom markets, and once FRHC gets the much-needed digital infrastructure in place, I think it won't be too hard to bring its superapp ecosystem to those new markets, thus explaining the TAM.

I also like the founder-led culture within the firm, and the fact that the CEO, Timur Turlov, reinvests most of the proceeds from his ~ 71% stake in FRHC back into the firm - this reflects a long-term, risk-tolerant growth strategy rather than short-term profit maximization like we see in some other fintech firms at the moment.

Based on all that, I'm reiterating my "Buy" on FRHC stock once again.

My Updated Reasoning

As I usually do, I'm going to start off with an update on FRHC's recent financials and corporate developments because the stock remains undercovered and hidden for the majority of Seeking Alpha readers (the stock has only 4,740 followers on SA as of today).

Anyway, in its fiscal Q1 2026 report, which was released in early August, Freedom showed $533.4 million in revenues (+17 YoY), and what I liked about it was the fact that they managed to reverse the negative impact from trading revenues last year, so this time the comparison was in their favor.

As surprising as it might seem to some of my Western readers, the financial market landscape in Kazakhstan is pretty competitive, so in order for FRHC to stay ahead of others, it must spend more OPEX and CAPEX and constantly develop simultaneously in multiple directions to diversify away at least some of its operational risks.

Despite lower fees and commission income for the quarter, FRHC's fee and commission expenses rose +5.9% YoY. Combined with other OPEX items that also increased meaningfully, the consolidated operating expense rose over 19%. The difference between revenue and OPEX growth rates led to some net earnings compression in Q1, and the diluted EPS figure went down by 12.28% YoY.

But I consider this weakness temporary because all those increased expenses led to great improvements in the holding's unit economics.

When we look at FRHC's banking division, we'll see 2.93 million clients as of June 30, 2025 (up from 2.52 million as of March 2025), so the growth of over 16% in the client base took place in a single quarter.

And when we take the whole ecosystem, we see that Freedom Holding now serves ~5.3 million people, up nearly 5% from March 2025, and it's about 26.5% of Kazakhstan's population. The customer acquisition costs that follow this massive expansion are totally worth it, in my view, and in the longer run, they'll likely pay off big as the ecosystem scales further.

The current ongoings at FRHC perfectly align with the CEO's vision for the future. I couldn't find FRHC's earnings calls on Seeking Alpha or elsewhere, so to gain a better understanding of that corporate vision, I think we should take a look at the recent interview with Timur Turlov that he gave to bne IntelliNews last month.

Without digging deep into his personal life story, which I also find quite interesting, I'd like to note that he seems to view FRHC as a "digital conglomerate" that seamlessly integrates different banking and non-banking services and maximizes "digital gravity."

Kazakhstan is the home market motherland of Freedom Holding, but with just 20 million people, it's a relatively small consumer market that Timur views as a real-world laboratory for testing the holding's business model. So far, it's been working exceptionally well - otherwise, we wouldn't see the stock going parabolic since its IPO.

But it's clear to me that once the needed scale of the business model is finally reached, and it's already close as far as I can see it, FRHC will be going to the neighboring selling markets like Kyrgyzstan, Uzbekistan, Georgia, etc. And we're already witnessing just that:

The Central Asian population is over 80 million people, which is four times bigger than in Kazakhstan. This figure in absolute terms may look small compared to the U.S. or China, but it has a median age of 26.7, 46% is urbanized, and importantly, the population is constantly growing by more than 1.5% annually. The prospective TAM is actually pretty promising for FRHC, and we also have to keep in mind that Turlov is unlikely to stop just in that region.

As he put it in the interview, he wants to "build bridges" between the West and the East, and so I think the next logical step for FRHC's ecosystem after the Central Asian end market is penetrated enough is the U.S. and Europe. And the firm is already represented there, although with limited offerings for now.

Now, let me update my previous valuation analysis on FRHC stock.

On a TTM basis, the stock trades at a P/E of over 123x because even though the earnings were hit by the rise in acquisition costs, the stock price didn't dip, and thus an extreme multiple expansion took place. The market is a forward-looking mechanism that tends to look beyond the TTM financials. That's why FRHC is actually a cheap stock, in my opinion - its forward P/E ratio is less than 10x, at the time of this writing.

With a wider client base, it's likely to get easier for FRHC to gain back its earnings and even show some growth over the previous periods. We don't have any particular guidance from the management on what's coming next year, but I think FRHC won't be expanding its client acquisition costs next year that much.

"Our super app helps us significantly lower customer acquisition cost," says Turlov. "I think we are now running the best scoring model in terms of consumer loans, small and medium business loans, insurance policies and life insurance."

Source: bne IntelliNews interview

So, if they manage to return to their fiscal FY2024 diluted EPS of $6.33, and gain 10% on top of that from the already-expanded client base, we'll see a P/E ratio at just ~24.27x. A further multiple expansion towards 30x would give FRHC an upside potential of 23.6%, if my math is correct.

Risks

Risks are there, and as I wrote last time on the stock, Freedom Holding is domiciled in Kazakhstan, and that's where its main serviceable market is located. Yes, they operate across the globe as well and target different regions in their operational expansion strategy, but they still make most of their money from Kazakhstan. So, we have to keep in mind that: a) the country risk isn't supposed to vanish any time soon, and b) any major macro downturn in that emerging country will naturally hurt the firm and its stock price. And in addition to all that, I think that the 30x earnings multiple that I took above as my base case scenario for FRHC stock's forward P/E target may be too much.

Your Takeaway

While FRHC is definitely not a riskless investment, I really like the corporate vision the holding's CEO has. It's very likely to translate to some tangible results, just like it has since the digital transformation that FRHC initiated relatively recently.

To me, the fact that Turlov keeps reinvesting his potential dividends back into the firm's growth is a great sign that FRHC can grow into something even bigger than its current $10.5 billion market cap. He could have just sold most of his shares to BlackRock or anyone else, but instead, he decided to follow the plan that was on the agenda for the past few years, and as a shareholder myself, I like to see that.

Seeing a potential upside of over 23% to the current stock price, I'm reiterating my "Buy" on FHRC once again.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: CleanSpark: Up 40% In Two Months, But The HPC-Driven Runway May Just Be Starting
data.description:
Following my last article on Cleanspark (NASDAQ:CLSK), the stock price appreciated by roughly 40%. This underscores significant gain over about two months, highlighting broader market outperformance. The stock price achieved $15.37 on October 1st, reaching my 12-month price target of $15.00 in 9 weeks.

Nevertheless, I think the momentum may continue with the company's announcement of expanding by an additional $100 million credit capacity to scale in Bitcoin mining operations and investing in high-performance computing. The market rewarded legacy Bitcoin miners — IREN and CIFR — which pivoted focus from solely mining operations to the HPC segment. Iris Energy increased by more than 500%, while Cipher Mining surged by more than 250% over the past 12 months.

I don't think you can fully compare these companies on a one-to-one basis, or expect the same outcome, but I believe CleanSpark's management pivot to diversify in HPC operations was the right decision to make, reducing dependency on Bitcoin cyclicality, broadening operations, and positioning in a high-growth cloud computing market.

I believe the market may reward CLSK with a higher premium because of these structural changes, and I don't think this is priced in yet. My bullish thesis remains unchanged; it remains as a Strong Buy, but I raise the 12-month price target from $15 to $18, justified by a more diversified business model, phenomenal execution from management, supported with adaptivity to market demand, and reduced dependency on Bitcoin, which weighs on the stock price.

FQ3 2025 Snapshot: Strong Beat On The Bottom Line, 90% YoY Revenue Growth, And Continued EH/s Expansion

On August 7, the company posted FQ3 2025 results. This was a strong quarter for the company, underscoring a well-run business, stability in operations, and significant improvement on a year-over-year basis, increasing investor confidence.

Although I estimated higher results in my previous article, they were suppressed by higher-than-anticipated depreciation and amortization costs and interest expense. Regardless, the company posted roughly $199 million in revenue, highlighting about a 90% increase on a year-over-year basis, and underscoring $1.7 million miss on the top-line versus analysts' estimates (0.85% surprise). The bottom line came in at $0.78, a substantial improvement from negative $1.03 in the same quarter last year, and $0.28 above analysts' expectations (36% surprise).

In the August 2025 investor presentation, the company highlighted $44,806 cost to mine, which, although increased from a roughly $43,000 estimate in the May 2025 presentation, still appears to be compelling and underlines well-positioning in the Bitcoin miner landscape. This also justifies the thesis of the company being profitable, which should help accelerate further growth in both mining and high-processing computing operations.

On September 3, the company disclosed its most recent Bitcoin mining update and underlined that it has achieved 12,827 BTC in its treasury. The Bitcoin mining company favors an operational approach to expand their BTC holdings rather than raise funds via debt instruments, which I think is the rational thing to do. Why would you buy BTC at the market price if your cost to mine allows you to acquire it at a significantly lower cost?

CleanSpark also highlighted that they expect to upgrade their mining fleet and fuel ongoing expansions. Although they haven't mentioned a specific estimate timewise, CLSK plans to onboard additional power capacity and increase 10 EH/s of incremental hashrate. This supports a bullish stance for the company, as it is positioning itself to benefit from an appreciated Bitcoin price, while mining at substantially lower costs.

Pro-Crypto Administration, Favorable Bitcoin Seasonality, And Pivoting To HPC

There are key tailwinds, which may result in a further stock price appreciation for CleanSpark. The current administration is pro-crypto, which helps establish more clearer regulatory framework, and improve sentiment in the landscape. On July 18, 2025, the US president signed the GENIUS Act, establishing a Federal regulatory system for stablecoins. I believe this will significantly contribute to the faster adoption of crypto assets.

Based on the report, only 28% of adults in the US own cryptocurrencies, yet the number has almost doubled from 15% in 2021. This implies that with more people adopting cryptocurrencies and equities with exposure to the crypto industry, it may fuel the appreciation of the underlying assets, and CLSK may significantly benefit from it. The miner has a substantial BTC treasury, and if the company continues to mine Bitcoin at approximately the current costs, the appreciation in the underlying may significantly improve margins.

The table below indicates that Q4 is a favorable season for Bitcoin. Historically, this crypto asset has appreciated by 79% on average since 2013, with roughly 48% appreciation on median terms. This highlights significant tailwinds tied to favorable BTC seasonality, which could boost the crypto prices and improve the sentiment of equities with crypto exposure. CleanSpark trades at a forward P/E of roughly 9.55 (GAAP), implying more than 3x discount to peers of 33, and could experience significant upside if crypto sentiment improves.

I also think that a pro-crypto administration supports the likelihood of a positive Q4 for the crypto landscape. Such regulatory acts, as GENIUS, highly contribute to a favorable environment, as it indicates investors shouldn't expect any significant obstacles for further crypto adoption.

Furthermore, there are more and more Bitcoin treasury companies, which are providing a floor price for the underlying asset and continuously expanding their portfolios. This highly contributes to a favorable Q4 thesis, and CLSK is well-positioned to benefit from the crypto appreciation. CleanSpark is also the 9th company in the list based on the total HODL, compared to other public companies following the BTC treasury capital strategy.

Given its recent announcement, CleanSpark is no longer a pure Bitcoin miner. The company decided to partially pivot to the HPC landscape, which currently experiences significant demand. The global HPC market was valued at $57 billion in 2024 and is expected to increase to about $87 billion by 2030. This implies a 7.2% CAGR and supports the bullish narrative for the stock.

The chart above indicates the stock price comparison of CLSK, IREN, and CIFR. As it reveals, those stocks were highly correlated until both IREN and CIFR pivoted to the HPC industry. Since then, both of the names significantly outperformed CleanSpark, although it has appreciated by nearly 70% over the past 12 months. Now it doesn't mean that CLSK will experience such a drastic move to the upside — but it supports the bullish stance, and shows that the market sees diversification to HPC as highly favorable for legacy Bitcoin miners.

Given the tailwinds, I think CleanSpark may experience stock appreciation in the following months and outperform the benchmark. The current environment appears to be favorable for equities with exposure to crypto. I think CLSK may appear compelling for long-term investors who seek to diversify in a high-growth name with increased focus on the global HPC market.

3x Undervaluation To Peers, Despite 70% 12-Month Rally

CleanSpark trades at a forward P/E of 9.55 (GAAP), which appears to be more than 3x below the sector median of 33.07. This highlights significant undervaluation, despite the company rallying about 70% over the past 12 months. This suggests that if the company continues to execute, there is room for multiple expansions.

I kindly invite you to read a more in-depth valuation analysis in the previous article, yet I would like to highlight a few important traits here.

The company trades at a forward EV/EBITDA of 8.40, indicating a roughly 46% discount to peers of 15.58. The forward Price/Book at 2.20 highlights more than 2x undervaluation versus the sector median of 4.52. This supports my thesis that the market has not yet priced in their pivot to HPC, and if the company continues to execute well, the market may reward them with multiple expansions.

CLSK remains a high-growth name, underscoring about 85% revenue growth over the past 12 months, substantially above the sector median of 7%. The EBITDA grew by 173%, and EBIT by 533% over the past year, representing a significant advantage compared to the peers of 10% and 12% growth, respectively. This also supports the bullish narrative for the company, and the market may reward phenomenal growth with a higher premium.

CleanSpark is a high-quality Bitcoin miner that operates at above-market margins. The gross profit margin (TTM) of 52% appears to be roughly 5% higher than peers of 50%, and the net income margin (TTM) of 48% underscores roughly 10x outperformance versus the sector median of 4%.

On the downside, the Bitcoin miner appears to be a medium-leveraged business with total obligations of $820 million. This is significant and resulted in $3.5 million in interest expense last quarter.

However, I think this could also act as a short-term tailwind for the stock. The Fed is expected to reduce interest rates by two additional 0.25% cuts by the end of the year. If this materializes, the company may be able to refinance its obligations on more favorable conditions that could lead to lower interest expense. This could meaningfully help bottom-line growth.

Overall, CleanSpark trades at a significant discount to peers. The company operates at above-market margins and grows its top line at an impressive pace. Management decided to expand into the high-demand HPC market, which supports the bullish stance for the miner, justified by diversification, reducing dependency on Bitcoin volatility, and following other legacy miners, which were significantly rewarded by the market.

This does not guarantee success. The company also appears to be medium-leveraged with significant obligations that could weigh on the stock price. Nevertheless, management does an excellent job by improving low-cost Bitcoin mining operations, increasing their BTC treasury, and now pivoting to the high-demand AI-driven HPC landscape, which could unlock a new growth catalyst for the stock.

Wall Street forecasts $1.53 in FY2025 EPS. I think this is a reasonable estimate. If the BTC price continues to appreciate and follows the seasonality, the company is well-positioned to beat the estimate. Nevertheless, it falls between the lower end of the estimates range of $1.15 to $2.57, indicating that more analysts lean toward a conservative approach.

I favor that too, as it leaves more room for error. Regardless, I think that $1.53 EPS is likely, and that would put the stock price at 9.55x earnings multiple. Given the analysis, it appears that the market has not yet priced in its pivot to HPC. AI names are usually rewarded with a higher premium and significant discount to peers, which implies that the company may experience upside with improved sentiment.

If we apply a 12x earnings multiple, which would still be roughly 2.8x below the sector median of 33x, and almost 2x below the average company in the S&P 500 at 23x, to FY2025 EPS of $1.53, we come in at my price target of $18. I think this is a reasonable estimate, which appears to be conservative, but also justifies the possibility of the stock price appreciation further.

I think CLSK is an interesting opportunity for investors seeking exposure in the HPC market. This is a volatile, high-growth name, but it is tied to multiple tailwinds that could drive the stock price further.

Risks And Concerns

There are other risks and concerns tied to CleanSpark, which were not mentioned in my previous analysis.

I think the main concern relates to CLSK's pivot to the HPC landscape. This shift toward AI demand may be appreciated by the market, but there are a lot of uncertainties about the move. It is not certain whether this is going to be a significant pivot; what are the revenue projections from HPC-related activities, and how fast CLSK can scale into the industry? Until those appear clearer, it is likely that CLSK may not be rewarded with a significant premium.

Although there are tailwinds tied to favorable seasonality, this may not result in substantial crypto appreciation over the next few months. If Bitcoin performs weaker-than-anticipated in the last quarter of the year, sentiment may shift, which could weigh on CleanSpark and continue trading at a significant discount.

If none of the above risks and concerns materialize, it appears CLSK is well-positioned to benefit from crypto tailwinds and AI-driven HPC demand. I think this could lead to the repricing of the stock at a higher multiple, which may result in an appreciation in the stock price.

Conclusion: HPC Pivot May Unlock A New Revenue Growth Catalyst

I believe CleanSpark offers a compelling opportunity for long-term investors who seek to diversify in a high-growth name, tied to AI demand, at undervalued levels. Although the stock price has increased roughly 70% over the past year, it appears to be trading at a significant discount versus peers, which supports the bullish outlook.

I think that Bitcoin miners' pivot to HPC was the right decision, but it is tied to execution risks. The company appears to be medium-leveraged, which could weigh on the stock price, but I think its tailwinds substantially overtake risks and concerns. Other miners who shifted to HPC were rewarded with significant upside, yet it does not mean CLSK will face the same success.

However, with this strategic move, the company diversifies its business, reduces dependency on Bitcoin volatility, which deserves a higher premium. My bullish thesis is unchanged. I think CleanSpark is a Strong Buy, with an $18 price target over the next 12 months, implying roughly 24% upside and broader market outperformance.

12 Wall Street analysts forecast a $20 12-month price target, suggesting roughly 38% upside. This is definitely a possibility, but I favor a conservative approach, which leaves me room for error.

data.title: Upside Inflation Bias Continues As Fed Faces Data Blackout
data.description:
By James Picerno

Consumers are concerned about prices and inflation, according to the latest survey by the Conference Board. The consultancy’s widely followed survey for September highlights weakening consumer confidence amid worries about prices and inflation, the latter being “the main topic influencing consumers’ views of the economy.”

It’s still debatable if the recent rebound in inflation in recent months will be a temporary, moderate affair, or an early sign of a new run of pricing pressures that will require a hawkish pivot in monetary policy. The Federal Reserve seems to be leaning toward the temporary/moderate view, based on the central bank’s decision to cut interest rates last month and prioritize a softer labor market as the higher priority.

A key update that is expected to provide timely clarification ahead of the next Fed meeting on October 29: September consumer inflation data, scheduled for release on October 15. Fed funds futures are currently pricing a near certainty that the central bank will ease policy again by 25 basis points at the end of the month.

The upcoming consumer price index (CPI) report could be crucial for deciding if further easing is appropriate, assuming the update is published. The government shutdown that started on Wednesday (October 1) threatens to complicate and cloud the Fed’s analysis by keeping the door locked at the Bureau of Labor Statistics and other federal agencies that publish economic data.

A quick resolution to the political impasse in Washington would keep the inflation updates on track, but there’s also a non-zero possibility that, given the level of partisan animosity, the government shutdown stretches into weeks. Since 1976, the government has shut down 20 times, the longest (in 2018-2019) lasting 34 days.

Whatever happens, the inflation outlook could remain cloudy until the full effects of tariffs emerge in the months ahead. Meantime, in an effort to separate the noise from signal in real time, TMC Research monitors several alternative inflation indexes published by two regional Fed banks to check on the robustness of the recent trend shift in the standard CPI data.

For example, the chart below compares core CPI’s 1-year trend against the TMC Core Inflation Index (CII), which is the median of five measures of prices that use varying methodologies to identify the underlying trend. The logic for this approach: any one inflation index has pros and cons, and so, reviewing a range of alternatives offers a more reliable measure of the pricing bias.

The key takeaway: CII is confirming that inflation’s upward bias is picking up, rising further above of the Fed’s 2% inflation target through August.

Core readings of inflation, which strip out food and energy, are useful because studies show that excluding those commodities delivers a more reliable measure of the trend vs. headline indexes that include those items, which tend to be relatively volatile and move with greater independence vis-à-vis changes in monetary policy.

On that basis, the faster pace and ongoing acceleration in CII suggests that inflationary momentum has further to run in year-over-year terms. If September inflation data reconfirms the trend (assuming the data is published), the case for another Fed rate cut will be harder to justify at the end of the month.

On the other hand, if hiring is still slowing in September, perhaps further policy easing is prudent. For the moment, however, the official government data is on hiatus, and so, private sector estimates are the only game in town. On that front, the results are mixed, according to two updates this week.

The ADP Employment Report for last month indicates that companies cut payrolls for a second straight month in September. By contrast, nonfarm payrolls rose 60,000 last, a moderate improvement over the previous month, according to estimates by Revelio Labs, a data analytics firm.

Here are the five price indexes used to calculate the TMC Core Inflation Index:

  • 16% Trimmed-Mean Consumer Price Index

  • Median Consumer Price Index

  • Sticky Price Consumer Price Index less Food and Energy

  • Sticky Price Consumer Price Index less Food, Energy, and Shelter

  • Sticky Price Consumer Price Index less Shelter

Original Source: The Milwaukee Company
